trans-unit.source: Projections
trans-unit.target: Projections

trans-unit.source: Introduction
trans-unit.target: Introduction

trans-unit.source: YouTube video player
trans-unit.target: YouTube video player

source.bpt: <iframe width="800" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/kIID5FDi2JQ?si=C0tYz7nteMF_xqvr" [#$sd1_sf5_tu2] frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen>

target.bpt: <iframe width="800" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/kIID5FDi2JQ?si=C0tYz7nteMF_xqvr" [#$sd1_sf5_tu2] frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen>

trans-unit.source: An important issue when creating a map of a region, is that it is impossible to create a representation of a sphere on a 2D plane without distorting the map. The transformation of a 3D object onto a flat surface can be done with the help of a __projection__. Over the centuries, cartographers and mathematicians have developed a multitude of different methods to project the earth onto a flat surface ({numref}`en_examples_projections_IBIS`). However, it is never possible to correctly represent the world on a flat surface (see the video above). Every projection distorts either the length between two points, the angles between two lines (directions), or the size of an area. A projection can only correctly represent one of these three dimensions. This means, that depending on the projection method, your world map will not represent the size, angles, or distances correctly.
trans-unit.target: An important issue when creating a map of a region, is that it is impossible to create a representation of a sphere on a 2D plane without distorting the map. The transformation of a 3D object onto a flat surface can be done with the help of a __projection__. Over the centuries, cartographers and mathematicians have developed a multitude of different methods to project the earth onto a flat surface ({numref}`en_examples_projections_IBIS`). However, it is never possible to correctly represent the world on a flat surface (see the video above). Every projection distorts either the length between two points, the angles between two lines (directions), or the size of an area. A projection can only correctly represent one of these three dimensions. This means, that depending on the projection method, your world map will not represent the size, angles, or distances correctly.

trans-unit.source: Examples for Projections (Source:Unknown. This figure is included for illustrative purposes only and is not subject to the Creative Commons license of this platform). :::
trans-unit.target: Examples for Projections (Source:Unknown. This figure is included for illustrative purposes only and is not subject to the Creative Commons license of this platform). :::

trans-unit.source: Every projection has its use case. For example, the Mercator projection displays the angles between to points correctly. This was used extensively during the seafaring age without satellites, as ships could navigate to a destination by following a straight line on a map. For example, the Mercator projection displays road intersections correctly: a road that crosses another road at a right angle, will be displayed as such on a mercator projection. This is especially useful when navigating. The shape of an area remains correct, since the angles between each line stay true. However, if you increase the scale of the map, the size and distances get distorted dramatically (see figure below). Furthermore, the further away from the equator you get, the more distortion you get.
trans-unit.target: Every projection has its use case. For example, the Mercator projection displays the angles between to points correctly. This was used extensively during the seafaring age without satellites, as ships could navigate to a destination by following a straight line on a map. For example, the Mercator projection displays road intersections correctly: a road that crosses another road at a right angle, will be displayed as such on a mercator projection. This is especially useful when navigating. The shape of an area remains correct, since the angles between each line stay true. However, if you increase the scale of the map, the size and distances get distorted dramatically (see figure below). Furthermore, the further away from the equator you get, the more distortion you get.

trans-unit.source: :::{note} The True Size of The mercator projection is famous for distorting the size of different countries. You can check the true size in comparison to different placements on the map on [TheTrueSize.com website](https://www.thetruesize.com).A popular example is Greenland in comparison with Africa, which seem on the map to be about the same size, but in reality Africa is a lot bigger. :::
trans-unit.target: :::{note} The True Size of The mercator projection is famous for distorting the size of different countries. You can check the true size in comparison to different placements on the map on [TheTrueSize.com website](https://www.thetruesize.com).A popular example is Greenland in comparison with Africa, which seem on the map to be about the same size, but in reality Africa is a lot bigger. :::

trans-unit.source: Comparison Greenland - Africa (Source: [The True Size of](https://www.thetruesize.com/#?borders=1~!MTYwODM1MTk.MzkyNDUyNg*MjY5NjM4Mzg(MTA1MjgyOTE~!CONTIGUOUS_US*MTAwMjQwNzU.MjUwMjM1MTc(MTc1)MQ~!IN*NTI2NDA1MQ.Nzg2MzQyMQ)MA~!CN*OTkyMTY5Nw.NzMxNDcwNQ(MjI1)Mg)). :::
trans-unit.target: Comparison Greenland - Africa (Source: [The True Size of](https://www.thetruesize.com/#?borders=1~!MTYwODM1MTk.MzkyNDUyNg*MjY5NjM4Mzg(MTA1MjgyOTE~!CONTIGUOUS_US*MTAwMjQwNzU.MjUwMjM1MTc(MTc1)MQ~!IN*NTI2NDA1MQ.Nzg2MzQyMQ)MA~!CN*OTkyMTY5Nw.NzMxNDcwNQ(MjI1)Mg)). :::

trans-unit.source: How to choose an appropriate projected coordinate system
trans-unit.target: How to choose an appropriate projected coordinate system

trans-unit.source: In GIS, we project the earth onto a flat coordinate system (hence the name coordinate reference system or CRS). It is crucial that you are aware that your data can be in one CRS and your QGIS project in another CRS.
trans-unit.target: In GIS, we project the earth onto a flat coordinate system (hence the name coordinate reference system or CRS). It is crucial that you are aware that your data can be in one CRS and your QGIS project in another CRS.

trans-unit.source: The project CRS is displayed on the bottom rightcorner of the [QGIS interface](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_1/en_qgis_start.html#overview-of-qgis-interface). Here, you can see the EPSG code. EPSG stands for European Petroleum Survey Group, and it refers to a standardized code system for coordinate reference systems (CRS) and projections. Each EPSG code (e.g., EPSG:4326 for WGS84) uniquely identifies a specific CRS, helping ensure consistency and interoperability in geospatial data across different platforms and applications.
trans-unit.target: The project CRS is displayed on the bottom rightcorner of the [QGIS interface](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_1/en_qgis_start.html#overview-of-qgis-interface). Here, you can see the EPSG code. EPSG stands for European Petroleum Survey Group, and it refers to a standardized code system for coordinate reference systems (CRS) and projections. Each EPSG code (e.g., EPSG:4326 for WGS84) uniquely identifies a specific CRS, helping ensure consistency and interoperability in geospatial data across different platforms and applications.

trans-unit.source: __EPSG Codes:__ These are numerical identifiers assigned by the EPSG database to specific coordinate reference systems, making them concise and unambiguous (e.g., EPSG:4326 for WGS84). They provide a standardized way to reference CRS across various GIS applications.
trans-unit.target: __EPSG Codes:__ These are numerical identifiers assigned by the EPSG database to specific coordinate reference systems, making them concise and unambiguous (e.g., EPSG:4326 for WGS84). They provide a standardized way to reference CRS across various GIS applications.

trans-unit.source: __CRS Names:__ These are typically descriptive names for coordinate reference systems (e.g., "WGS 84" or "NAD83"). While names can provide insight into the system being used, they may not be unique or universally recognized, leading to potential confusion without the accompanying EPSG code.
trans-unit.target: __CRS Names:__ These are typically descriptive names for coordinate reference systems (e.g., "WGS 84" or "NAD83"). While names can provide insight into the system being used, they may not be unique or universally recognized, leading to potential confusion without the accompanying EPSG code.

trans-unit.source: To change the CRS of your data and project, follow the steps explained below. The default CRS/EPSG code of every QGIS project is the World Geodetic System 84 (EPSG: 4326). This CRS is optimized for world maps and therefore is not ideal for most humanitarian applications, as we need region-specific projections that provide the least distortion on the scale we wish to represent.
trans-unit.target: To change the CRS of your data and project, follow the steps explained below. The default CRS/EPSG code of every QGIS project is the World Geodetic System 84 (EPSG: 4326). This CRS is optimized for world maps and therefore is not ideal for most humanitarian applications, as we need region-specific projections that provide the least distortion on the scale we wish to represent.

trans-unit.source: Look at the following images and pay attention to how the different Coordinate Reference Systems change and distort the world map.
trans-unit.target: Look at the following images and pay attention to how the different Coordinate Reference Systems change and distort the world map.

trans-unit.source: The Mercator Projection (EPSG:54004) (Source: HeiGIT). :::
trans-unit.target: The Mercator Projection (EPSG:54004) (Source: HeiGIT). :::

trans-unit.source: Notice how the shape of the circle stays the same. Out of this, we can conclude that the angles stay the same. However, the circles get bigger the further away they are from the equator, and the distance between these circles change the further they get from the equator. Therefore, we can conclude that the distances and sizes are being distorted with the mercator projection. The strength of the Mercator projection is that it conserves the angles between two lines. We can see this because the circles stay perfectly circular the further they are from the equator.
trans-unit.target: Notice how the shape of the circle stays the same. Out of this, we can conclude that the angles stay the same. However, the circles get bigger the further away they are from the equator, and the distance between these circles change the further they get from the equator. Therefore, we can conclude that the distances and sizes are being distorted with the mercator projection. The strength of the Mercator projection is that it conserves the angles between two lines. We can see this because the circles stay perfectly circular the further they are from the equator.

trans-unit.source: The World Geodetic System 1984 (EPSG:4326) (Source: HeiGIT). :::
trans-unit.target: The World Geodetic System 1984 (EPSG:4326) (Source: HeiGIT). :::

trans-unit.source: The WGS 84 is a CRS which consists of an ellipsoid that closely resembles the shape of the earth. Instead of metrical units of measurements, it uses angular degrees (latitude and longitude). The shape of the Tissot circles is undistorted near the equator, but becomes elongated on the East-West axis the further it gets away from the equator. Unlike the Mercator projection, there is no distortion in the North-South direction. As the circles become distorted, we can deduce that this CRS distorts the angles.
trans-unit.target: The WGS 84 is a CRS which consists of an ellipsoid that closely resembles the shape of the earth. Instead of metrical units of measurements, it uses angular degrees (latitude and longitude). The shape of the Tissot circles is undistorted near the equator, but becomes elongated on the East-West axis the further it gets away from the equator. Unlike the Mercator projection, there is no distortion in the North-South direction. As the circles become distorted, we can deduce that this CRS distorts the angles.

trans-unit.source: The World Equidistant Cylindrical Projection (EPSG:54002) (Source: HeiGIT). :::
trans-unit.target: The World Equidistant Cylindrical Projection (EPSG:54002) (Source: HeiGIT). :::

trans-unit.source: The World Equidistant cylindrical CRS is equidistant (not distorting the length) along any meridian (circles of longitude; North to South), and along the two standard parallels. The shape, scale and area distort the further they are away from the standard parallels.
trans-unit.target: The World Equidistant cylindrical CRS is equidistant (not distorting the length) along any meridian (circles of longitude; North to South), and along the two standard parallels. The shape, scale and area distort the further they are away from the standard parallels.

trans-unit.source: This table shows an overview on which projections to use for which needed characteristic:
trans-unit.target: This table shows an overview on which projections to use for which needed characteristic:

trans-unit.source: Characteristic
trans-unit.target: Characteristic

trans-unit.source: Mercator (cylindrical)
trans-unit.target: Mercator (cylindrical)

trans-unit.source: Lambert cylindrical
trans-unit.target: Lambert cylindrical

trans-unit.source: Albers conic
trans-unit.target: Albers conic

trans-unit.source: Shape
trans-unit.target: Shape

trans-unit.source: Rotation
trans-unit.target: Rotation

trans-unit.source: Area
trans-unit.target: Area

trans-unit.source: Another very important consideration when choosing the Coordinate reference system is that, depending on the ellipsoid and the method used to project the same point can be located at different locations (see {numref}`wrong_CRS`). In the figure below, the same point is encoded in 3 different reference systems.
trans-unit.target: Another very important consideration when choosing the Coordinate reference system is that, depending on the ellipsoid and the method used to project the same point can be located at different locations (see {numref}`wrong_CRS`). In the figure below, the same point is encoded in 3 different reference systems.

trans-unit.source: The same point in three different reference systems (Source: HeiGIT). :::
trans-unit.target: The same point in three different reference systems (Source: HeiGIT). :::

trans-unit.source: Metric and Geographic Coordinate Reference Systems
trans-unit.target: Metric and Geographic Coordinate Reference Systems

trans-unit.source: There are two different types of Coordinate Reference System: __Geographic__ or __Metric__ CRS.
trans-unit.target: There are two different types of Coordinate Reference System: __Geographic__ or __Metric__ CRS.

trans-unit.source: A Geographic CRS is based on a three-dimensional, ellipsoidal model of the Earth. It uses angular measurements (__latitude and longitude__) to define locations on the Earth's surface. The coordinates are usually expressed in degrees (e.g., 45°N, 120°W).
trans-unit.target: A Geographic CRS is based on a three-dimensional, ellipsoidal model of the Earth. It uses angular measurements (__latitude and longitude__) to define locations on the Earth's surface. The coordinates are usually expressed in degrees (e.g., 45°N, 120°W).

trans-unit.source: __Advantages__: Since it is based on the Earth's curvature, it can be used to represent locations anywhere on the earth. Most global datasets, GPS, and mapping systems use Geographic CRS making it highly compatible with various data sources. Locations can be specified accurately with angular measurements.
trans-unit.target: __Advantages__: Since it is based on the Earth's curvature, it can be used to represent locations anywhere on the earth. Most global datasets, GPS, and mapping systems use Geographic CRS making it highly compatible with various data sources. Locations can be specified accurately with angular measurements.

trans-unit.source: __Disadvantage__: Because it uses angular measurements, distances, areas, and shapes can be highly __distorted__. Since the distance between the circles of latitude and longitude change, the conversion of angles to meters is not constant
trans-unit.target: __Disadvantage__: Because it uses angular measurements, distances, areas, and shapes can be highly __distorted__. Since the distance between the circles of latitude and longitude change, the conversion of angles to meters is not constant

trans-unit.source: A metric CRS is a 2D representation of the earths surface. Although it is difficult to represent large areas of the globe on a 2D surface without surface, it is possible to create a 2D projection of a limited region with minimal distortion. The map units are typically metres or feet. It is created by projecting the earth onto a flat plane.
trans-unit.target: A metric CRS is a 2D representation of the earths surface. Although it is difficult to represent large areas of the globe on a 2D surface without surface, it is possible to create a 2D projection of a limited region with minimal distortion. The map units are typically metres or feet. It is created by projecting the earth onto a flat plane.

trans-unit.source: __Advantages__: Since it uses a flat plane, you can calculate distances, areas, and angles accurately.
trans-unit.target: __Advantages__: Since it uses a flat plane, you can calculate distances, areas, and angles accurately.

trans-unit.source: __Disadvantages__: A given projected CRS is usually optimized for a particular region. Using it outside its intended area can lead to significant distortions in distance, area, and shape.
trans-unit.target: __Disadvantages__: A given projected CRS is usually optimized for a particular region. Using it outside its intended area can lead to significant distortions in distance, area, and shape.

trans-unit.source: A geographic representation of the globe. The distance between the meridians converges towards the north and south pole (Source: HeiGIT). :::
trans-unit.target: A geographic representation of the globe. The distance between the meridians converges towards the north and south pole (Source: HeiGIT). :::

trans-unit.source: When processing geodata, QGIS always uses the units of measurements of the layer that you are processing. This means that if you want to calculate, for example, the distance in kilometers, the layer must be in a metric CRS. You can check the units of measurements of any given layer by <kbd>Right clicking</kbd> on the layer in the layer panel → `Properties` → `Information` → `Coordinate Reference System (CRS)`.
trans-unit.target: When processing geodata, QGIS always uses the units of measurements of the layer that you are processing. This means that if you want to calculate, for example, the distance in kilometers, the layer must be in a metric CRS. You can check the units of measurements of any given layer by <kbd>Right clicking</kbd> on the layer in the layer panel → `Properties` → `Information` → `Coordinate Reference System (CRS)`.

trans-unit.source: Local and Global CRS
trans-unit.target: Local and Global CRS

trans-unit.source: Local and global coordinate reference systems (CRS) (Source: British Red Cross). :::
trans-unit.target: Local and global coordinate reference systems (CRS) (Source: British Red Cross). :::

trans-unit.source: As you can see, smaller regions look skewed and distorted in a global CRS. For smaller areas local projections should be used, since they give a more accurate display. However, local projections heavily distort the map on a global level.
trans-unit.target: As you can see, smaller regions look skewed and distorted in a global CRS. For smaller areas local projections should be used, since they give a more accurate display. However, local projections heavily distort the map on a global level.

trans-unit.source: How to check and change the project coordinate reference system
trans-unit.target: How to check and change the project coordinate reference system

trans-unit.source: :::{admonition} Now it's your turn! :class: note
trans-unit.target: :::{admonition} Now it's your turn! :class: note

trans-unit.source: Understanding projections and coordinate reference systems is not easy. The next steps can be followed with any geodata layer in your QGIS project.
trans-unit.target: Understanding projections and coordinate reference systems is not easy. The next steps can be followed with any geodata layer in your QGIS project.

trans-unit.source: Open a QGIS project.
trans-unit.target: Open a QGIS project.

trans-unit.source: In the very bottom right corner of QGIS you find the button `EPSG`. The number next to it is the EPSG Code currently used in the project. To see more information, or to change the CRS, click on the `Current CRS`-button ![](/fig/EPSG_Code.png).
trans-unit.target: In the very bottom right corner of QGIS you find the button `EPSG`. The number next to it is the EPSG Code currently used in the project. To see more information, or to change the CRS, click on the `Current CRS`-button ![](/fig/EPSG_Code.png).

trans-unit.source: The window `Project Properties` will open. Here you can view all available CRS/EPSG-Code and their properties.
trans-unit.target: The window `Project Properties` will open. Here you can view all available CRS/EPSG-Code and their properties.

trans-unit.source: To change the CRS/EPSG code, select the one you want to use and click `Apply`.
trans-unit.target: To change the CRS/EPSG code, select the one you want to use and click `Apply`.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Video: How to check and change the CRS in your QGIS project
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Video: How to check and change the CRS in your QGIS project

trans-unit.source: Project CRS and Layer CRS
trans-unit.target: Project CRS and Layer CRS

trans-unit.source: The coordinate reference system of your QGIS project determines how QGIS displays the information. However, layers and datasets have their own CRS. This can be seen in the metadata, or layer properties of the dataset. The layer CRS refers to the coordinate system of the features or items in the dataset. The same coordinates in two different coordinate reference systems do not refer to the same location on earth. This is because of the distortion of distance and area.
trans-unit.target: The coordinate reference system of your QGIS project determines how QGIS displays the information. However, layers and datasets have their own CRS. This can be seen in the metadata, or layer properties of the dataset. The layer CRS refers to the coordinate system of the features or items in the dataset. The same coordinates in two different coordinate reference systems do not refer to the same location on earth. This is because of the distortion of distance and area.

trans-unit.source: Changing the projection of a vector layer
trans-unit.target: Changing the projection of a vector layer

trans-unit.source: `Vector` Tab → `Data Management Tools` → `Reproject Layer`.
trans-unit.target: `Vector` Tab → `Data Management Tools` → `Reproject Layer`.

trans-unit.source: Select target CRS/EPSG code.
trans-unit.target: Select target CRS/EPSG code.

trans-unit.source: Save the new file by clicking on the three dots next to `Reprojected`, specify the file name and the location where you want to save the file.
trans-unit.target: Save the new file by clicking on the three dots next to `Reprojected`, specify the file name and the location where you want to save the file.

trans-unit.source: Click `Run`.
trans-unit.target: Click `Run`.

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Video: How to change the CRS of a vector a layer
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Video: How to change the CRS of a vector a layer
:::

trans-unit.source: Changing the projection of a raster layer
trans-unit.target: Changing the projection of a raster layer

trans-unit.source: `Raster` Tab → `Projections` → `Warp (Reproject)`.
trans-unit.target: `Raster` Tab → `Projections` → `Warp (Reproject)`.

trans-unit.source: Select target CRS/EPSG-Code.
trans-unit.target: Select target CRS/EPSG-Code.

trans-unit.source: Select resampling method.
trans-unit.target: Select resampling method.

trans-unit.source: Save the new file by clicking on the three dots next to `Reprojected`, specify the file name and the location where you want to save the file.
trans-unit.target: Save the new file by clicking on the three dots next to `Reprojected`, specify the file name and the location where you want to save the file.

trans-unit.source: Click `Run`.
trans-unit.target: Click `Run`.

trans-unit.source:
:::{dropdown} Video: How to change the CRS of a raster layer
:::
trans-unit.target:
:::{dropdown} Video: How to change the CRS of a raster layer
:::

trans-unit.source: Self-Assessment Questions
trans-unit.target: Self-Assessment Questions

trans-unit.source: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note
trans-unit.target: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note

trans-unit.source: Take a moment to test what you've learned in this chapter by answering the questions below:
trans-unit.target: Take a moment to test what you've learned in this chapter by answering the questions below:

trans-unit.source: __Why is a map projection needed when visualizing the Earth in 2D? What distortions are inevitable when you project a spherical surface to a plane?__
trans-unit.target: __Why is a map projection needed when visualizing the Earth in 2D? What distortions are inevitable when you project a spherical surface to a plane?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Because the Earth (or more precisely the reference ellipsoid) is a three‑dimensional curved surface, you cannot represent it perfectly on a flat (2D) map without distortion. The process of “projecting” transforms the curved surface into a plane.
trans-unit.target: Because the Earth (or more precisely the reference ellipsoid) is a three‑dimensional curved surface, you cannot represent it perfectly on a flat (2D) map without distortion. The process of “projecting” transforms the curved surface into a plane.

trans-unit.source: Any projection necessarily distorts at least one of: angles (directions), areas (relative sizes), or distances (scales). You can often preserve one or two properties well, but not all simultaneously.
trans-unit.target: Any projection necessarily distorts at least one of: angles (directions), areas (relative sizes), or distances (scales). You can often preserve one or two properties well, but not all simultaneously.

trans-unit.source: For example, the Mercator projection preserves angles (so shapes are locally correct), which is useful for navigation, but severely distorts areas (especially towards the poles).
trans-unit.target: For example, the Mercator projection preserves angles (so shapes are locally correct), which is useful for navigation, but severely distorts areas (especially towards the poles).

trans-unit.source: Another example: equidistant projections preserve distances along particular lines (meridians or standard parallels), but distort shape or area in other parts. :::
trans-unit.target: Another example: equidistant projections preserve distances along particular lines (meridians or standard parallels), but distort shape or area in other parts. :::

trans-unit.source: __Explain the difference between a Geographic CRS and a Projected (or Metric) CRS. What are their advantages and disadvantages?__ :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: __Explain the difference between a Geographic CRS and a Projected (or Metric) CRS. What are their advantages and disadvantages?__ :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: A Geographic CRS (Coordinate Reference System) uses an ellipsoidal (or spherical) model of the Earth and expresses locations via angular coordinates (latitude, longitude) — generally in degrees.
trans-unit.target: A Geographic CRS (Coordinate Reference System) uses an ellipsoidal (or spherical) model of the Earth and expresses locations via angular coordinates (latitude, longitude) — generally in degrees.

trans-unit.source: Advantages: It naturally reflects the Earth’s curvature; it works globally (you can locate anywhere on the globe). Many global datasets, GPS systems, and maps are inherently in geographic (lat/long) form, so it provides compatibility.
trans-unit.target: Advantages: It naturally reflects the Earth’s curvature; it works globally (you can locate anywhere on the globe). Many global datasets, GPS systems, and maps are inherently in geographic (lat/long) form, so it provides compatibility.

trans-unit.source: Disadvantages: Because latitude/longitude are angular, converting them to linear measures (meters, kilometers) is non‑uniform. Distances, areas, and shapes can be distorted, especially away from the equator. You can’t reliably measure Euclidean distances or areas without first projecting
trans-unit.target: Disadvantages: Because latitude/longitude are angular, converting them to linear measures (meters, kilometers) is non‑uniform. Distances, areas, and shapes can be distorted, especially away from the equator. You can’t reliably measure Euclidean distances or areas without first projecting

trans-unit.source: A Projected (Metric) CRS transforms the Earth’s surface (or part of it) into a two‑dimensional plane, with linear units (metres, feet, etc.). The projection “flattens” the surface.
trans-unit.target: A Projected (Metric) CRS transforms the Earth’s surface (or part of it) into a two‑dimensional plane, with linear units (metres, feet, etc.). The projection “flattens” the surface.

trans-unit.source: Advantages: Because the data are in a planar metric space, you can compute distances, areas, and angles more directly and with less distortion (within the projection’s intended extent). It’s better for most spatial analyses (buffering, measuring) when the area is limited.
trans-unit.target: Advantages: Because the data are in a planar metric space, you can compute distances, areas, and angles more directly and with less distortion (within the projection’s intended extent). It’s better for most spatial analyses (buffering, measuring) when the area is limited.

trans-unit.source: Disadvantages: Each projected CRS is usually optimized for a particular region or zone. Outside that region, distortion increases (in shape, scale, or area). A single projection cannot serve uniformly well over the entire globe. Also, projections may introduce distortions in directions or area even within their domain.
trans-unit.target: Disadvantages: Each projected CRS is usually optimized for a particular region or zone. Outside that region, distortion increases (in shape, scale, or area). A single projection cannot serve uniformly well over the entire globe. Also, projections may introduce distortions in directions or area even within their domain.

trans-unit.source: __Why is it important to choose a CRS appropriate to your area of interest (local vs global)? What problems may arise if you apply a local CRS to global data (or vice versa)?__
trans-unit.target: __Why is it important to choose a CRS appropriate to your area of interest (local vs global)? What problems may arise if you apply a local CRS to global data (or vice versa)?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Because every projection is optimized to reduce distortion in some aspect (area, distance, shape) over a particular spatial extent (region). For a local area, a local or regionally tuned CRS will minimize distortion and allow more accurate measurements.
trans-unit.target: Because every projection is optimized to reduce distortion in some aspect (area, distance, shape) over a particular spatial extent (region). For a local area, a local or regionally tuned CRS will minimize distortion and allow more accurate measurements.

trans-unit.source: If you apply a local CRS (meant for a small area) to global data, distortions become extreme outside the intended region. Features may be skewed, stretched, or mislocated, and distance/area computations will be highly inaccurate in regions far from the projection’s optimal zone.
trans-unit.target: If you apply a local CRS (meant for a small area) to global data, distortions become extreme outside the intended region. Features may be skewed, stretched, or mislocated, and distance/area computations will be highly inaccurate in regions far from the projection’s optimal zone.

trans-unit.source: Conversely, using a global projection (e.g. a world Mercator) for a local region may not exploit the possibility of reducing distortion, and may even yield worse local accuracy than a more specialized projection. Also, global projections often distort large areas—so local detail may suffer.
trans-unit.target: Conversely, using a global projection (e.g. a world Mercator) for a local region may not exploit the possibility of reducing distortion, and may even yield worse local accuracy than a more specialized projection. Also, global projections often distort large areas—so local detail may suffer.

trans-unit.source: In summary: applying a mismatched CRS leads to inconsistencies, misalignment of layers, errors in measurements, and misleading spatial relationships.
trans-unit.target: In summary: applying a mismatched CRS leads to inconsistencies, misalignment of layers, errors in measurements, and misleading spatial relationships.

trans-unit.source: __What is an EPSG code, and how is it useful in selecting or referring to a CRS?__
trans-unit.target: __What is an EPSG code, and how is it useful in selecting or referring to a CRS?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: EPSG stands for the European Petroleum Survey Group, which maintains a registry (database) of coordinate reference systems and projections.
trans-unit.target: EPSG stands for the European Petroleum Survey Group, which maintains a registry (database) of coordinate reference systems and projections.

trans-unit.source: An EPSG code is a numeric identifier (e.g. EPSG:4326) assigned to a particular CRS.
trans-unit.target: An EPSG code is a numeric identifier (e.g. EPSG:4326) assigned to a particular CRS.

trans-unit.source: It is useful because it provides a standardized, unambiguous way to refer to a CRS across GIS software and datasets. Rather than relying solely on possibly ambiguous names, the EPSG code ensures that the exact CRS (ellipsoid, projection parameters, units) is identified.
trans-unit.target: It is useful because it provides a standardized, unambiguous way to refer to a CRS across GIS software and datasets. Rather than relying solely on possibly ambiguous names, the EPSG code ensures that the exact CRS (ellipsoid, projection parameters, units) is identified.

trans-unit.source: When selecting a CRS in QGIS or other GIS tools, you often search by EPSG code or filter by region. The EPSG.io website is a useful resource to look up CRS codes appropriate for your region.
trans-unit.target: When selecting a CRS in QGIS or other GIS tools, you often search by EPSG code or filter by region. The EPSG.io website is a useful resource to look up CRS codes appropriate for your region.

trans-unit.source: __How do you reproject (change the CRS) of a vector layer in QGIS?__
trans-unit.target: __How do you reproject (change the CRS) of a vector layer in QGIS?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: In the top bar, navigate to the `Vector` menu → `Data Management Tools` → `Reproject Layer`.
trans-unit.target: In the top bar, navigate to the `Vector` menu → `Data Management Tools` → `Reproject Layer`.

trans-unit.source: In the parameters window, select the target CRS (by searching the EPSG code or name).
trans-unit.target: In the parameters window, select the target CRS (by searching the EPSG code or name).

trans-unit.source: Click `Run`. A new layer called `Reprojected` will be added to the map canvas.
trans-unit.target: Click `Run`. A new layer called `Reprojected` will be added to the map canvas.

trans-unit.source: Further resources
trans-unit.target: Further resources

trans-unit.source: The website [__I Hate Coordinate Systems!__](https://ihatecoordinatesystems.com/) offers a “a problem-based guide of common CRS issues, root causes, and solutions”. Check it out in case you have any issues with CRS.
trans-unit.target: The website [__I Hate Coordinate Systems!__](https://ihatecoordinatesystems.com/) offers a “a problem-based guide of common CRS issues, root causes, and solutions”. Check it out in case you have any issues with CRS.
